title: Committing to Steady Compute Spend
description:
## Maximum Discount With Maximum Flexibility

A company runs a **steady, 24/7 production baseline** of compute that has been stable for a year and is expected to continue. The catch: over the next term they expect to **change instance families and sizes**, shift some workloads from EC2 to Fargate, and possibly move between Regions as they re-architect. They want the **largest possible discount over On-Demand** while keeping the **maximum flexibility** to make those changes without losing the discount. The baseline is not interruptible.

options.text: Purchase Compute Savings Plans for the steady baseline
Purchase Standard Reserved Instances for specific instance types in a specific Region
Run everything on Spot Instances to get the deepest discount
Stay entirely On-Demand to retain flexibility

explanation: **Compute Savings Plans** give RI-level discounts (commit to a $/hour spend for 1 or 3 years) while automatically applying across **any instance family, size, Region, OS, tenancy, and even Fargate and Lambda**. That is the best fit when the baseline is steady (so committing is safe) but the team needs freedom to change families, move to Fargate, and shift Regions without re-buying — the discount follows their usage. **Standard RIs** (B) offer a similar or slightly larger discount but are far less flexible: they are tied to instance attributes/Region and don't cover Fargate/Lambda, so re-architecting could strand them. **Spot** (C) gives the deepest discount but instances can be reclaimed with a 2-minute warning — unsuitable for a non-interruptible production baseline. **On-Demand** (D) is fully flexible but the most expensive, giving up the discount entirely.

hints.title: Flexibility is the deciding axis
hints.body: Two options give big discounts on steady usage. The tie-breaker is which one survives changing instance family, Region, and a move to Fargate.

hints.title: Interruptible?
hints.body: The baseline is explicitly not interruptible — that single fact removes Spot from contention regardless of its discount.
